title: Pre-attentive Vision
summary: The human visual system processes certain attributes in under 250ms, before conscious thought. Color, size, shape, orientation, and motion are detected in parallel across the entire visual field.
rules: Use color or size to create instant hierarchy — the eye lands there first
Limit pre-attentive signals to 1-2 per screen to avoid visual noise
Use motion (animation) sparingly — it hijacks attention completely
Combine multiple pre-attentive cues (color + size) for critical CTAs
dos: Use a single accent color for your primary action
Make the most important number or stat visually largest
Use a distinct shape or icon for error/warning states
donts: Don't use more than 5 colors in a UI — each competes for attention
Don't animate decorative elements — reserve motion for meaningful feedback
Don't rely on position alone to imply importance

title: Gestalt Principles
summary: The brain groups visual elements automatically using rules: proximity (close = related), similarity (look alike = same group), closure (fills in gaps), continuity (follows lines), and figure-ground (separates object from background).
rules: Use whitespace (proximity) to group related elements — no border needed
Use consistent visual treatment for items in the same category (similarity)
Cards and containers leverage closure — they group without hard lines
Align elements on a grid to leverage continuity
dos: Group form fields by function using spacing, not boxes
Use consistent icon style across the whole UI
Create sections with whitespace rather than horizontal rules
donts: Don't mix icon styles (outline vs filled) in the same context
Don't use borders to group things that proximity would handle
Don't place unrelated items close together

title: Cognitive Load & Miller's Law
summary: Working memory holds 4±1 chunks at a time (Miller's Law updated). Every element on screen that requires interpretation consumes a chunk. When overloaded, users freeze, leave, or make errors.
rules: Limit primary navigation to 5-7 items
Break long forms into steps — one decision per screen
Use progressive disclosure: show advanced options only on demand
Remove any element that doesn't directly serve the user's current goal
dos: Use chunking: group phone numbers as (555) 123-4567, not 5551234567
Show only the fields relevant to the current step
Use defaults intelligently to reduce required decisions
donts: Don't show all configuration options at once
Don't use jargon that requires mental translation
Don't make users remember information from a previous screen

title: Visual Hierarchy
summary: The order in which the eye travels across a composition. Controlled by size, weight, color, contrast, and spacing. A strong hierarchy makes the path obvious; a weak one forces the user to hunt.
rules: One element should clearly dominate — the primary CTA or headline
Use 3 levels of hierarchy max: primary, secondary, tertiary
Increase contrast for more important elements
Size differences of at least 1.5x to feel intentional
dos: Make the page title or hero statement the largest text element
Use font weight (bold vs regular) as a hierarchy signal
Reduce opacity for supporting text, not just size
donts: Don't make everything bold — it cancels out
Don't use the same font size for labels and values
Don't treat all CTAs as equal — one must be primary

title: The Squint Test
summary: If you squint at your UI until it blurs, the primary element should still be obvious. If you can't tell what the page is about when blurred, the hierarchy is broken.
rules: The most important element should have the most visual mass
CTA buttons must contrast strongly with their background
Headers should be noticeably larger than body copy
dos: Test your design at 50% zoom — does it still make sense?
Ensure your primary CTA has 4.5:1 contrast ratio minimum
donts: Don't use subtle color differences for critical state changes
Don't rely on hover states to reveal important actions

title: Fitts's Law
summary: Time to acquire a target = log2(distance/size + 1). Bigger targets and shorter travel distances reduce errors and frustration. Small clickable elements far from the user's focus create friction.
rules: Primary CTAs should be large (min 44×44px) and near the content they act on
Destructive actions (delete, cancel) should be small and distant
Navigation items should be edge-anchored or consistently positioned
Mobile: all touch targets minimum 48dp (Google) / 44pt (Apple)
dos: Make the entire card clickable, not just a small 'View' link
Place the primary button at the natural end of the user's reading path
Use full-width buttons in mobile modals
donts: Don't use tiny close buttons (X) on critical modals
Don't put destructive buttons next to primary actions
Don't shrink touch targets to save space

title: Hick's Law
summary: Decision time = log2(n + 1) where n = number of choices. More options = longer decisions = more abandoned sessions. Reducing choice is a feature, not a limitation.
rules: Limit navigation to 5-7 top-level items
Offer a recommended option to shortcut the decision
Remove rarely-used options from primary UI — put them in Settings
For onboarding: wizard beats a list of features
dos: Pre-select the most popular or recommended plan/option
Use 'Most Popular' or 'Recommended' badges
In search results, limit visible results to 5-10 with a 'See all'
donts: Don't give equal visual weight to all navigation items
Don't list every feature — lead with the top 3 benefits
Don't make users choose before they understand the options

title: Typography & Readability
summary: Typography accounts for 95% of web design. Line length, leading, weight contrast, and hierarchy determine whether text gets read or skipped.
rules: Line length: 60-80 characters (45-75ch) for body text
Line height: 1.5-1.7x for body, 1.1-1.3x for headings
Minimum body text: 16px (14px minimum for secondary copy)
Font pairing: max 2 typefaces — one for headings, one for body
dos: Use system fonts or a well-known variable font for performance
Increase font size on mobile (16px minimum)
Use real typographic hierarchy: H1 > H2 > body is visually distinct
donts: Don't use more than 2 font families
Don't use all-caps for body text
Don't set line height below 1.4 for body copy
Don't center-align long text blocks

title: Color & Contrast (WCAG)
summary: Color contrast isn't just accessibility compliance — it's the primary driver of legibility. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text. 300 million people have color vision deficiency.
rules: Normal text: minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio (WCAG AA)
Large text (18px+ bold or 24px+): minimum 3:1
Never use color as the only signal — pair with shape, text, or pattern
Test all states: default, hover, focus, disabled, error
dos: Use a contrast checker on every text/background combination
Pair color with icons for status (error = red + X icon)
Offer a high-contrast mode or ensure base design is WCAG AA
donts: Don't use light gray text on white background
Don't use only color to indicate error state
Don't use red/green as the only distinction (8% of men have color blindness)

title: F-Pattern & Reading Patterns
summary: Eye-tracking studies show users scan in an F-shape on content-heavy pages: full first line, partial second line, then left edge. The right side of the screen below the fold is largely ignored.
rules: Place the most important content in the top-left quadrant
Front-load key information in headings and first sentences
Use left-aligned text for scannable lists
Break F-pattern with images, callouts, or visual interrupts
dos: Put your primary CTA in the top-right or end of the first line
Use bold or callout boxes to interrupt the F scan
Front-load bullet points with the key word
donts: Don't put critical information in the right column (it will be missed)
Don't bury the lead — lead with the most important point
Don't use long dense paragraphs — users scan, not read

title: Affordance & Signifiers
summary: An affordance is what an object can do. A signifier is the visual cue that communicates it. Buttons need depth cues, links need underlines or color, inputs need borders. Without signifiers, users don't click.
rules: Buttons must look distinctly different from non-interactive text
Links should be colored and/or underlined — don't style them like plain text
Form inputs need a visible border or background distinction
Disabled states must look obviously inactive (reduced opacity)
dos: Use border-radius, shadows, or gradient to signal button depth
Show cursor:pointer on all interactive elements
Add hover states to all clickable elements
donts: Don't create false affordances — don't make non-clickable things look clickable
Don't rely on color alone to distinguish links from text
Don't remove button borders on white backgrounds

title: Feedback Latency & Doherty Threshold
summary: Every action must produce a reaction within 400ms (Doherty Threshold). 0.1s = instantaneous, 1s = seamless flow, 10s = attention lost. Without feedback, users assume failure and click again.
rules: Show visual feedback within 100ms of any click/tap
Provide loading indicators for anything over 400ms
Use optimistic UI — show success state before server confirms
Disable buttons during loading to prevent double submission
dos: Add button loading states (spinner or text change) on form submit
Use skeleton screens instead of spinner for content loading
Show inline validation as user types, not only on submit
donts: Don't leave buttons in their default state during async operations
Don't use page refreshes for operations that can be async
Don't delay feedback to 'feel more real' — faster is always better

title: Anchoring Bias
summary: The first piece of information seen acts as a calibration point for all subsequent judgments. First number seen dominates price perception. First option seen sets the baseline for feature comparison.
rules: Show your highest-tier option first to anchor expectations
Use a decoy option to make the target tier look reasonable
Display original price before discounted price (crossed out)
Feature the 'recommended' tier with visual prominence
dos: Put the most expensive plan on the left in pricing tables
Show MSRP before your price — even if it's industry standard pricing
Use 'Save X%' badges to reinforce the anchor comparison
donts: Don't show cheapest option first if you want to upsell
Don't present all options with equal visual weight
Don't hide the value comparison — make anchoring explicit

title: The Halo Effect (Aesthetic-Usability Effect)
summary: A single positive trait (visual beauty) biases perception of unrelated traits (trustworthiness, competence, security). Users rate beautiful interfaces as more usable, even with identical functionality. Sloppy spacing erodes trust.
rules: Consistent spacing signals competence — use an 8px grid
Font choice signals brand personality before a word is read
First-load impression sets the entire perception of the product
Error states and edge cases still need polish — they're trust moments
dos: Use a design system or token system to ensure spacing consistency
Polish micro-interactions — they signal attention to detail
Ensure onboarding/signup flows look as good as marketing pages
donts: Don't launch with inconsistent spacing or misaligned elements
Don't use system-default form styles if your brand depends on trust
Don't let internal tools look broken — it signals poor craftsmanship

title: Progressive Disclosure
summary: Show only what's needed for the current task. Reveal complexity progressively as the user's intent becomes clear. Novice and expert paths should coexist without one degrading the other.
rules: Show 'Advanced' or 'More options' toggles instead of always-visible settings
Onboarding should ask minimum required info — get more detail over time
Expandable sections work better than tabs for settings pages
dos: Use a wizard pattern for complex multi-step flows
Defer optional configuration to after the user achieves first value
Reveal contextual options (edit, delete) only on hover/focus
donts: Don't show power-user options to new users on their first visit
Don't ask for information before it's needed
Don't collapse information that users need frequently

title: Color Psychology & Palette Strategy
summary: Colors carry universal and cultural meaning that sets emotional tone before content is read. Blue = trust/technology, Green = success/growth/health, Red = urgency/danger, Purple = premium/creative, Orange = energy/action, Black = luxury/power.
rules: Choose your primary color based on the emotional contract with your user
Limit palette to: 1 primary, 1 secondary/accent, neutrals, semantic (error/success/warning)
Dark mode requires separate contrast validation — don't just invert
Semantic colors (red=error, green=success) must be culturally consistent
dos: Use your primary color for the most important interactive element only
Test your palette with a color blindness simulator
Define your palette as design tokens for consistency
donts: Don't use more than 5 distinct colors in a single view
Don't use color trends that clash with your brand personality
Don't use pure black (#000) — use near-black (#111, #1a1a1a) for better readability
